---
title: "Variable Ordering and COLAMD"
---

## Variable Ordering

The natural question after seeing fill-ins is: **which variable should
we eliminate first?** Getting this order right turns out to matter a
great deal for performance.

### Neighbours

Suppose the graph looks like:

$$
x_1 - x_2 - x_3
$$

The neighbours of $x_2$ are $x_1$ and $x_3$:

$$
N(x_2) = \{x_1, x_3\}
$$

### Elimination Rule

**When eliminating a variable, connect all of its neighbours together.**

So after eliminating $x_2$ from the graph above, the result is:

$$
x_1 - x_3
$$

with a new edge appearing directly between $x_1$ and $x_3$ — this is
exactly the [fill-in from before](sparsity.qmd#where-the-new-edges-come-from).

**A bigger example:** suppose $N(x_1) = \{x_2, x_3, x_4\}$, arranged as:

$$
\begin{array}{c} x_2 \\ | \\ x_3 - x_1 - x_4 \end{array}
$$

Eliminating $x_1$ connects all three neighbours together:

$$
\begin{array}{c} x_2 \\ / \; \backslash \\ x_3 - x_4 \end{array}
$$

### Clique

A **clique** is a set of vertices where every pair is connected.
Formally, for $C = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k\}$:

$$
C \text{ is a clique if } (v_i, v_j) \in E \; \forall\, i \neq j
$$

### The Fundamental Theorem of Elimination

> **When eliminating a variable, its neighbours become a clique.**

This single fact is the root cause of fill-ins — every elimination step
forces all of the eliminated variable's neighbours to become fully
interconnected.

### Why Are Cliques Bad?

Here's a concrete example. Suppose $x$ has four neighbours $n_1, n_2,
n_3, n_4$:

$$
\begin{array}{c} n_2 \\ | \\ n_1 - x - n_3 \\ | \\ n_4 \end{array} \quad (4 \text{ edges})
$$

Eliminating $x$ turns $\{n_1, n_2, n_3, n_4\}$ into a clique — every
pair now connected:

$$
\binom{4}{2} = 6 \text{ edges}
$$

**The graph became denser.** In general, if a variable has $k$
neighbours, eliminating it creates:

::: {.callout-important}
## Fill-in Growth Is Quadratic

$$
\binom{k}{2} = \frac{k(k-1)}{2} \text{ edges}
$$

The growth is quadratic in $k$, which escalates fast:

- 2 neighbours → 1 edge
- 100 neighbours → 4950 edges → **computational disaster**
:::

> **We should eliminate variables with smaller degree first** — not
> because the math changes, but purely as a computational trick to keep
> the graph as sparse as possible for as long as possible.

## Minimum Degree Ordering

We've established that eliminating low-degree variables first keeps
fill-in under control — this strategy is called **Minimum Degree
Ordering**. But there's a catch: **choosing the elimination order is
itself an NP-hard problem.** We can't just try every possible ordering
and pick the best one — there are $n!$ possibilities.

Worse, since removing one variable changes the graph, a naive algorithm
would need to **recompute degrees after every single elimination**.
That's very expensive at scale.

### Approximate Minimum Degree (AMD)

The practical fix: don't compute the exact degree every time. Instead,
**estimate** how many neighbours a node is likely to have after
fill-in, and use that estimate to guide the ordering.

AMD doesn't always produce the *optimal* ordering — but it produces a
**good** one, cheaply enough to actually be usable on large problems.

## COLAMD: Column Approximate Minimum Degree

COLAMD takes this a step further with an important shift in
perspective: **it optimizes the ordering on the Jacobian, not the
Hessian.**

This matters because the Hessian $H$ can already have [fill-ins baked
into it](sparsity.qmd#fill-ins-during-factorization) — working directly
from the Jacobian avoids inheriting that problem.

### Why "Column"?

Recall the structure of the Jacobian: every **factor** is a **row**,
and every **variable** is a **column**. COLAMD works by **reordering
the columns** — i.e., reordering the variables. Critically, this
reordering never changes the underlying math, only the order in which
variables get eliminated.

### How It Works

Suppose we have a Jacobian $J$ whose columns correspond to variables
$[x_1, x_2, x_3, l_1]^T$:

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix}
* & * & 0 & 0 \\
0 & * & * & 0 \\
0 & 0 & * & * \\
* & 0 & 0 & *
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1: Convert the Jacobian to a bipartite graph.**

This is a graph with two types of nodes — rows are one type of node,
columns are another. Each factor row connects to the columns
(variables) it involves:

$$
f_1 \to \{x_1, x_2\}, \quad f_2 \to \{x_2, x_3\}, \quad f_3 \to \{x_3, l_1\}, \quad f_4 \to \{x_1, l_1\}
$$

**Step 2: Compute column degree** — how many *other* columns share a
row with this one.

For example, column $x_1$ appears in rows $f_1$ and $f_4$. Row $f_1$
also contains $x_2$, and row $f_4$ also contains $l_1$. So $x_1$ is
related to $\{x_2, l_1\}$:

$$
\therefore \; \text{approximate degree of } x_1 = 2
$$

This column-degree estimate is exactly the quantity COLAMD uses to
decide which variable to eliminate first — lower approximate degree
means eliminating that variable is less likely to trigger a cascade of
expensive fill-ins.
